title: Java Concurrency — Threads, Locks & Synchronisation
description: 20 questions on threads, synchronisation, volatile, deadlocks, and java.util.concurrent. High-value interview topic.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
questions.optionA: A process is lighter than a thread
questions.optionB: Threads within a process share the same memory space; processes have separate memory
questions.optionC: A thread can contain multiple processes
questions.optionD: Processes are managed by the JVM; threads are managed by the OS
questions.explanation: Threads within the same process share heap memory, static variables, and open files. Processes have isolated memory — one process cannot directly access another's memory. This makes thread communication faster but also riskier (shared state = race conditions).

questions.questionText: What does the volatile keyword guarantee in Java?
questions.optionA: Atomicity of compound operations like i++
questions.optionB: Mutual exclusion
questions.optionC: Visibility — writes to a volatile variable are immediately visible to all threads
questions.optionD: Both atomicity and visibility
questions.explanation: volatile guarantees visibility only — every write is flushed to main memory, every read comes from main memory. It does NOT guarantee atomicity. i++ on a volatile int is still NOT thread-safe because it is a read-modify-write (3 operations).

questions.questionText: Which of these creates a new thread in Java?
questions.optionA: new Thread(() -> {}).run()
questions.optionB: new Thread(() -> {}).start()
questions.optionC: Thread.create(() -> {})
questions.optionD: new Runnable(() -> {}).start()
questions.explanation: start() creates a new OS thread and calls run() in that thread. Calling run() directly just executes the Runnable on the current thread — no new thread is created. Runnable itself has no start() method.

questions.questionText: What condition is required for a deadlock to occur?
questions.optionA: Two threads sharing the same lock
questions.optionB: A thread holding lock A waiting for lock B, while another thread holds lock B waiting for lock A
questions.optionC: A thread sleeping for too long
questions.optionD: Too many threads running concurrently
questions.explanation: Deadlock requires 4 conditions (Coffman): mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait. The classic example is Thread 1 holds A, wants B; Thread 2 holds B, wants A — circular dependency, both block forever.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between synchronized method and synchronized block?
questions.optionA: No difference — they are identical
questions.optionB: synchronized method locks the entire object; synchronized block can lock a specific object
questions.optionC: synchronized block is faster but less safe
questions.optionD: synchronized method works on static methods only
questions.explanation: A synchronized method locks 'this' (the entire object) for the method's duration. A synchronized block gives fine-grained control — you choose which object to lock and only lock during the critical section. Smaller lock scope = less contention = better throughput.

questions.questionText: What does ReentrantLock offer that synchronized does not?
questions.optionA: Better performance in all cases
questions.optionB: tryLock(), timed lock acquisition, interruptible lock waiting, and fairness
questions.optionC: Automatic deadlock prevention
questions.optionD: Works across multiple JVMs
questions.explanation: ReentrantLock adds: tryLock() (non-blocking attempt), tryLock(timeout) (timed), lockInterruptibly() (can be interrupted while waiting), and fairness option (threads served in order). synchronized has none of these — it's simpler but less flexible.

questions.questionText: What is a race condition?
questions.optionA: A condition where threads compete for CPU time
questions.optionB: A bug where the program output depends on the relative timing/ordering of threads
questions.optionC: A performance issue caused by too many threads
questions.optionD: A deadlock between exactly two threads
questions.explanation: A race condition occurs when multiple threads access shared data concurrently and the final result depends on the order of execution. Classic example: two threads doing counter++ simultaneously — both read 0, both write 1, counter ends up as 1 instead of 2.

questions.questionText: What does CountDownLatch do in Java?
questions.optionA: Counts threads and terminates them when the count reaches zero
questions.optionB: Allows one or more threads to wait until a set of operations (counted down by other threads) complete
questions.optionC: Limits the number of concurrent threads
questions.optionD: Counts the number of deadlocks in a program
questions.explanation: CountDownLatch is initialized with a count. Threads call await() to block. Other threads call countDown() to decrement. When count reaches 0, all waiting threads are released. Unlike CyclicBarrier, CountDownLatch cannot be reused.

questions.questionText: Which ExecutorService method blocks until all submitted tasks complete?
questions.optionA: executor.shutdown()
questions.optionB: executor.shutdownNow()
questions.optionC: executor.awaitTermination(long, TimeUnit)
questions.optionD: executor.waitAll()
questions.explanation: Pattern: executor.shutdown() signals no new tasks, then executor.awaitTermination(timeout, unit) blocks until all tasks finish or timeout expires. shutdownNow() attempts to cancel running tasks. There is no waitAll() method.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between Callable and Runnable?
questions.optionA: Callable runs in a separate thread; Runnable does not
questions.optionB: Callable can return a result and throw checked exceptions; Runnable cannot
questions.optionC: Runnable is newer than Callable
questions.optionD: Callable requires an ExecutorService; Runnable does not
questions.explanation: Runnable.run() returns void and cannot throw checked exceptions. Callable.call() returns a generic type V and can throw Exception. Use Callable when you need the task's result via Future.get().

questions.questionText: What happens when you call Future.get() before the task completes?
questions.optionA: Returns null immediately
questions.optionB: Throws TimeoutException
questions.optionC: Blocks the calling thread until the task completes
questions.optionD: Cancels the task
questions.explanation: Future.get() is a blocking call — the current thread waits until the Callable finishes. Use get(timeout, unit) to avoid blocking indefinitely. If the Callable threw an exception, get() wraps it in ExecutionException.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the happens-before relationship in the Java Memory Model?
questions.optionA: It defines the physical execution order of bytecode
questions.optionB: It guarantees that actions in one thread are visible to another thread in a predictable way
questions.optionC: It prevents deadlocks by ordering lock acquisitions
questions.optionD: It is a scheduling policy for the JVM thread scheduler
questions.explanation: The Java Memory Model defines happens-before: if A happens-before B, then all memory writes by A are visible to B. Key rules: synchronized release happens-before acquire; volatile write happens-before volatile read; thread.start() happens-before the thread's first action.

questions.questionText: What is a ThreadLocal variable?
questions.optionA: A variable that can only be accessed from the main thread
questions.optionB: A variable where each thread has its own independent copy
questions.optionC: A variable shared between all threads but with synchronised access
questions.optionD: A variable stored in thread-local cache for performance
questions.explanation: ThreadLocal provides thread-isolated storage. Each thread that accesses a ThreadLocal variable gets its own initialised copy — no synchronisation needed. Common use: per-thread database connections, user sessions in web applications.

questions.questionText: Which concurrent collection allows concurrent reads without locking?
questions.optionA: Collections.synchronizedMap()
questions.optionB: Hashtable
questions.optionC: CopyOnWriteArrayList
questions.optionD: LinkedBlockingQueue
questions.explanation: CopyOnWriteArrayList creates a new copy of the underlying array on every write. Reads require no locking at all — they always see a consistent snapshot. Best for read-heavy, write-rare scenarios (e.g. listener lists).

questions.questionText: What is the difference between wait() and sleep() in Java?
questions.optionA: sleep() releases the lock; wait() does not
questions.optionB: wait() releases the lock and waits for notify(); sleep() keeps the lock and pauses
questions.optionC: They are identical — both pause the thread
questions.optionD: wait() is for threads; sleep() is for processes
questions.explanation: wait() must be called inside a synchronized block and releases the monitor lock while waiting — other threads can enter the synchronized block. sleep() does NOT release any locks — the thread just pauses with its locks held. wait() is woken by notify()/notifyAll(); sleep() wakes after the specified time.

questions.questionText: What is a Semaphore used for?
questions.optionA: Detecting deadlocks
questions.optionB: Controlling access to a resource pool by limiting the number of concurrent accessors
questions.optionC: Synchronising exactly two threads
questions.optionD: Broadcasting messages between threads
questions.explanation: Semaphore(n) allows up to n threads to acquire permits simultaneously. acquire() decrements the count (blocks if 0); release() increments it. Common use: connection pool of 10 connections — Semaphore(10) ensures at most 10 concurrent users.

questions.questionText: What does AtomicInteger.compareAndSet(expected, update) do?
questions.optionA: Sets the value to update only if it is currently equal to expected — atomically
questions.optionB: Compares two integers and returns true if equal
questions.optionC: Throws an exception if the current value differs from expected
questions.optionD: Locks the integer during the comparison
questions.explanation: CAS (Compare-And-Swap) atomically: if currentValue == expected, set to update and return true; else return false. This is the foundation of lock-free programming — no mutex needed for simple counters and flags.

questions.questionText: What is a livelock?
questions.optionA: A deadlock where threads are still running
questions.optionB: Threads that keep responding to each other but neither makes progress
questions.optionC: A thread that has been waiting for more than 10 seconds
questions.optionD: A situation where all threads are in the RUNNABLE state
questions.explanation: In a livelock, threads are active (not blocked) but keep reacting to each other without making progress. Classic example: two people in a corridor stepping aside simultaneously in the same direction indefinitely. Unlike deadlock, CPU is being consumed but no work is done.

questions.questionText: What is the recommended thread pool size for CPU-bound tasks?
questions.optionA: 1 thread
questions.optionB: Number of available CPU cores
questions.optionC: Number of CPU cores × 2
questions.optionD: As many threads as tasks
questions.explanation: For CPU-bound tasks, use Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors() threads. More threads just cause context-switching overhead with no benefit. For I/O-bound tasks (waiting on network/disk), you can use more threads (cores × 2 or higher) since threads spend time blocked.

questions.questionText: What state does a thread enter after calling synchronized on a locked object?
questions.optionA: TERMINATED
questions.optionB: WAITING
questions.optionC: BLOCKED
questions.optionD: TIMED_WAITING
questions.explanation: A thread enters the BLOCKED state when it tries to acquire a monitor lock that another thread holds. WAITING is entered via wait(), join(), or LockSupport.park(). TIMED_WAITING is entered via sleep(n), wait(n), or join(n). TERMINATED means the thread has completed.
